channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Peru’s Photo-Finish Election
item.description: The presidential race could hinge on just a few thousand votes.

item.title: War and Circuses
item.description: Trump juggles Iran, a World Cup, and a UFC cage fight.

item.title: Trump Backpedals on Planned Iran Strikes, Citing Deal Progress
item.description: But Iranian news reports that Tehran has not yet agreed to a deal.

item.title: Big Tech Gets Bigger
item.description: Recent stock-market gyrations are a reminder of just how dependent the United States and global economies have become on investors’ seemingly insatiable appetite for tech stocks. Even if a major correction is in the offing, however, it will not happen before SpaceX’s massive initial public offering draws in yet more leveraged investment, nor will it destroy the market power of the other tech giants that have spent years perfecting anti-competitive tactics that largely escape regulators’ purview.

item.title: The Big Picture
Hormuz Isn’t Closed Anymore. But It’s Not Fully Open, Either.
item.description: The Trump administration is borrowing Russia’s “shadow fleet” tactics to get ships moving.

item.title: When the Beautiful Game Meets the Ugliest Ego
item.description: A recent history of the World Cup suggests the tournament can triumph over Trump.

item.title: Washington’s Asian Allies Need a Backup Plan
item.description: A strategic rethink is needed in an era of U.S. unreliability.

item.title: Iran Is Now More Dangerous Than Ever
item.description: After three months of war, Tehran feels it has little left to lose.

item.title: Why Was Xi Jinping in North Korea?
item.description: The seven-year gap since Xi’s previous visit to North Korea suggests a cooling in bilateral relations. What does it mean that he made the trip to Pyongyang at last?

item.title: The best way to celebrate America at 250 is to get behind the wheel
item.description: Preferably with our new podcast series for company

item.title: Japan is rethinking its divorce laws
item.description: A move to introduce joint custody marks a big shift

item.title: In China ride-hailing work is a last resort for rural labourers
item.description: But now Shenzhen says it has too many of them

item.title: China’s notorious university-entrance exam is changing
item.description: Demography and AI are set to transform the gaokao

item.title: Tik-Tocqueville
item.description: Why “Democracy in America” is worth going back to on the country’s 250th birthday

item.title: Social media is behind both “teen takeovers” and the outrage they fuel
item.description: Cities struggle to respond to big gatherings of young folk

item.title: America’s quintessential places are getting old, fast
item.description: Welcome to the greying suburbs

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
Ukraine is transplanting its industrial heart to the west
item.description: Soviet-built factory towns are in the wrong spot now that Russia is the enemy

item.title: Another new boss aims to fix the world’s biggest chocolate-maker
item.description: Will Hein Schumacher’s turnaround plan for Barry Callebaut work?

item.title: Too many people are shockingly bad at prioritisation
item.description: Choosing where to focus is among the most important skills

item.title: The world’s wealthy are migrating like never before
item.description: A booming industry of advisers is easing their passage

item.title: Fighting in Mogadishu risks making a weak state weaker
item.description: A presidential power grab has convulsed Somali politics

item.title: Could Eritrea come in from the cold?
item.description: Isaias Afwerki, its reclusive dictator, has spotted an opening to reconcile with America

item.title: The first-ever robotic rescue at sea is a milestone
item.description: An American drone recovers downed pilots in the Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Iran has lost its fear of war
item.description: A once-cautious regime is making a risky bet on low-level conflict

item.title: Techno-libertarians are flocking to the Caribbean
item.description: Angry locals are trying to shut them down

item.title: How Tuvalu Is Rewriting the Rules of Statehood
item.description: As rising seas threaten its territory, the Pacific nation is leading an unprecedented effort to ensure sovereignty can survive climate change.

item.title: Sudan’s Rapid Support Forces Are Terrorists
item.description: Designating the group as such could help bring an end to Sudan’s civil war.

item.title: Climate Change and the Future of Central Asia-South Asia Connectivity
item.description: The region’s ambitious connectivity and energy projects were conceived in a different climatic reality.

item.title: The Democrats Need a Story, Not More Lists
item.description: Whereas Donald Trump has offered voters a clear and compelling narrative, Democrats have provided only vague aspirations and long lists of policies. To win over voters—rather than scrounging protest votes—they must articulate a compelling economic vision, with wage-led growth as its foundation.

item.title: Bangladesh’s Myanmar Border Challenge Is No Longer Just About the Rohingya
item.description: Bangladeshis are being abducted and killed by the Arakan Army, the de facto authority in areas along the border. But Dhaka can’t use official channels to talk to a non-state actor.

item.title: Can India’s cockroach party become a political movement?
item.description: The government is betting that the answer is no

item.title: Asian activists say too much egg production is cruel
item.description: They are hatching ingenious schemes

item.title: How Soccer Became ‘Un-American’
item.description: The sport is a marker of a particular relationship to the wider world.

item.title: Mexico Could Be the World Cup’s Biggest Winner
item.description: As the United States closes itself off from the world, its co-host is doing the opposite.

item.title: FIFA’s Versailles Moment
item.description: Soccer is egalitarian. The 2026 FIFA World Cup is anything but.

item.title: What if Europe Loses Access to Its Climate Data?
item.description: The European Green Deal runs on data, from corporate disclosures to climate-risk analyses and decarbonization pathways. But much of this data feeds platforms and tools developed outside the European Union, which could threaten the bloc’s climate agenda and even its foundational values.

item.title: How big are China’s emerging industries?
item.description: Probably not big enough to offset the drag from the old

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The year America reckoned with AIDS
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on how activists forced the country to confront the crisis

item.title: For its own sake, China should change its growth model
item.description: It is suffering economic costs for its industrial dominance

item.title: Why Modi’s Great Nicobar Project is a Recipe for Disaster
item.description: It will open the Andaman and Nicobar Islands to loot and plunder. It involves felling 15 million trees and encroaching upon the land of the reclusive Shompen tribals.

item.title: A trade war between the EU and China seems inevitable
item.description: Europe sees Chinese subsidies, China sees European weakness

item.title: Raising Interest Rates Won’t Stabilize African Inflation
item.description: Monetary tightening in Africa would not only fail to rein in inflation; it would raise the cost of productive investment, suppress growth, and hamper the continent's much-needed structural transformation. A better strategy would focus on accelerating this transformation by channeling finance into productive industries.

item.title: Stears wants to be Africa’s Bloomberg terminal
item.description: As the continent’s markets mature, high-quality financial analysis is more essential than ever

item.title: The world’s strategic oil reserves are running out fast
item.description: They cannot hold back the energy crunch for ever

item.title: A guide to redistributing AI wealth
item.description: From Trump to Sam Altman, the idea is catching on. Does it make sense?

item.title: How to share AI riches
item.description: From Donald Trump to Sam Altman, the idea of redistributing them is catching on. Does it make sense?

item.title: The Hegseth Shock for New Zealand
item.description: Accusations of defense “freeloading” hit the third rail of Wellington’s politics.

item.title: Ukraine’s war is now longer than the first world war
item.description: The ghost of Versailles haunts Ukraine’s peace

item.title: Peak Oil Demand Has Arrived
item.description: Amid the disruptions caused by the US/Israeli-Iran war, countries have begun adapting to life with less oil. What started as a supply shock has become a demand story, forcing the energy sector to reconsider one of its most important assumptions: continued growth of global oil demand.

item.title: The Knicks represent New York—and capitalism—at its best
item.description: That is why New Yorkers love them

item.title: Entertainment is being deglobalised
item.description: Why monoculture events like the World Cup are becoming the exception

item.title: Why UMNO is the Biggest Beneficiary of Malaysia’s PAS-Bersatu Divorce
item.description: The fracturing of the opposition camp could give Malaysia's grand old party a springboard back to federal prominence.

item.title: Why the Indo-Pacific Trade Hinges on East Africa
item.description: The region's ports, sea lanes, and gas fields have become an important part of geopolitical competition, from the Gulf to the Pacific Ocean.

item.title: Philippine Foreign Secretary Says She Will Meet Myanmar Opposition Groups Soon
item.description: The announcement comes as ASEAN member states move to normalize their relations with the new "civilian" government in Naypyidaw.

item.title: Silicon Valley’s Bad Bet on the Gulf
item.description: Why the AI build-out was doomed from the start.

item.title: The Real Problem With Global Trade
item.description: How China’s currency manipulation is warping the world economy.

item.title: Philippines Urges China to Remove ‘Movable Platform’ at Disputed South China Sea Shoal
item.description: The country's coast guard said that the structure, which first appeared on satellite images on May 25, may be supporting unauthorized marine research.

item.title: The World Cup paradox
item.description: How the rules of both entertainment and soft power are being rewritten

item.title: Patent Trolls and What’s Next in Tech Competition with China
item.description: The country's share of global patent filings has grown rapidly in recent years, part of a deliberate strategy to make IP and patents a new tool of economic competition.

item.title: What the Hormuz Energy Crisis is Teaching Taiwan About Marine Insurance
item.description: Taipei’s energy supply is secured for now, but it faces a separate concern of whether insurers will cover the ships that are needed to deliver it.

item.title: Democratizing Technology: India Stack 3.0 and the Future of Digital Public Infrastructure
item.description: Dr. Pramod Varma joins host Tushar Shetty to discuss the design philosophy and the next stages of India's Digital Public Infrastructure.

item.title: Australia-Germany 2+2 Ministerial Highlights an Expanding Network of Partners
item.description: Canberra likely hopes that its relationship with Berlin will come to hold a similar importance as those with Washington, London, and Tokyo.
